sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 7, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 has a gray background with: - 3 charcoal-framed blue 2x2 squares (targets?): at ~[24,44], ~[28,16], ~[36,32] - A horizontal bar object (blue frame with gray interior) at center - A white+green object at bottom (~row 48-51, col 32-35) - likely the player - Pink bar at bottom edge - HUD/timer
- Goal model: Navigate the green player to interact with the blue-square targets.
- Plan: Probe movement to understand controls, then figure out the objective.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 6 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed. Let me check what happened after UP. The animation changed bbox was [32, 32, 43, 35], so changes happened in that region. Let me check the current state.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state after UP
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

print("Current small objects:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] <= 30:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check blue squares specifically
blue_squares = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 4]
print(f"\nBlue squares: {len(blue_squares)}")
for bs in blue_squares:
    print(f"  boundary={bs['boundary']}")

# Check player position
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'N']:
        print(f"  Player part: color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After UP:
- The white frame (node 7) moved up from [36,32]-[40,35] to [32,32]-[36,35] (moved up 4 rows)
- The green player part (node 9) moved up from [41,32]-[43,35] to [37,32]-[39,35] (moved up 4 rows)
- The blue square (node 8) moved from [37,33]-[38,34] to [33,33]-[34,34] (moved up 4 rows, staying inside the white frame)
So the entire player structure (white frame + green body + blue square inside) moved up together. The blue square is being carried inside the white frame.
This means:
- SPACE "picks up" the target (the charcoal frame turns white and the blue square gets captured inside)
- The player can then carry the captured blue square around
- The goal is likely to collect all 3 blue squares and bring them somewhere
Now the player is at row 32-39, col 32-35, carrying one blue square. The other two targets are:
- Target 1: at [24,44]-[27,47] (top right)
- Target 2: at [28,16]-[31,19] (left)
I need to navigate to these targets. But first, let me understand the movement better. The player moves 4 rows per UP/DOWN. What about LEFT/RIGHT? Let me also check if I can move through the horizontal bar obstacle.
The horizontal bar (node 4, blue frame with gray interior) is at [28,28]-[31,39]. The player is at col 32-35, so part of the player overlaps with this bar horizontally. But the player is at row 32-39 now, while the bar is at row 28-31. So they're vertically adjacent.
Let me try moving LEFT to reach target 2 at col 16-19.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated:
- SPACE "captures" a target: charcoal frame turns white, blue square gets trapped inside.
- Player now carries the captured blue square (moved with it when going UP).
- Player at row 32-39, col 32-35, carrying 1 blue square.
- 2 remaining targets: top-right [24,44] and left [28,16].
- Goal: likely collect all 3 blue squares.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try LEFT to move horizontally
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
